All right, welcome back. So in this one, I'm going to show you how to maximize an e-commerce sales process. So by e-commerce, I mean like a business that sells stuff, you know, like water bottles, which Natalia just ordered this very strange water from an e-commerce store. It costs a lot of money, therefore it must be very, very good for us. It's oxygenated. So that's right there on the bottle, right? So this applies to you if you're selling t-shirts, headbands, makeup, vitamins, whatever. E-com sales processes are generally way simpler than like, you know, what we've been through and what I've shown you with the one-to-many and the professional services and all of that kind of stuff. And the thing about e-com, where you're going to make your money, is number one, getting the ads to convert, getting the right people over to your site and all of that, which we'll deal with later. But number two, it's going to be in two areas. Actually three is going to be on-site conversion, making your website actually convert better. And even more importantly, or something that's easier to control at least, is average order value. Actually getting people to spend more money with you when they're checking out and when they're buying from the store. So what I want to walk you through in this video is how to do that, give you the process map and the checklist and the usual cool stuff. Are you ready? Let's get to it. Sometimes I think we should all just specialize in e-com for reasons you're about to see, because great googly moogly, is there some low hanging fruit in terms of opportunity to make a really big impact with their type of businesses. So what do I mean by e-com? Well, people who sell real stuff on the internet, I mean, I'm sure you kind of know already, like makeup and stuff, vitamins, pet supplies, kung fu gear, things like that, you know, stuff that's sold on Shopify, etc. Now, the thing about an e-com business is typically they got a higher volume, meaning they're doing a whole lot of transactions, but their profit margins are a lot lower when compared to information products and professional services. And their big pain points are usually stuff like this happens. They've been running ads for a while and now it's costing them more and more and more to get a customer. And then as a result of that, their profits are getting smaller so you could have someone do in $10 million a year in an e-com business and growth sales and they might be netting like taking home 10% of that, you know, so it's a totally different game than information and services. And what's great about these businesses is they're really relatively easy to fix because the overwhelming majority of them don't do like super basic glaringly obvious stuff to maximize their profits. So, sort of god, no kidding, this is a typical e-com sales process. You got an ad, right, and it's either going to go to a product specific landing page or it's going to go to a presale page. I probably should have a little arrow there, but the ad is going to drive traffic to one of these two types of pages. All right. And then, so, you know, they go to the presale page, they'll either click over to the product or not, or the ad goes straight to the product page and then they go to a checkout page with some options, maybe, and that's it. The end forever. Swear to god, no kidding. we're taken to this page, which is great, okay, sweet, pretty easy, right, it asked me for a username and password on a screen before this, but it also just let me continue as guest, so I clicked this, and this is basically my shipping address, email and phone number, right, that's it, so there's no credit card yet, so step one of a two-step order form, all right, and then I go here, okay, and so now I could have purchased or not, I chose not to purchase just to see what would happen, guys, guess what happened, absolutely nothing, oh, incidentally here we go, I can review my order by the way, so I didn't even like, let me show you one more time, all right, so I got here, and so you click on review order, I can't even buy yet, I click on review order, then I see this, then I can buy, right, so I didn't buy, it just like confirmed all the stuff that I'd already typed in, you know, like let me give you a 37-step order process, and then I did nothing, all right, zero follow-up whatsoever, okay, nothing at all, and this is super common by the way, like e-com folks, they just think differently than us, like they're all really, really front-end driven, let me get more traffic, cheaper clicks, cheaper costs to get a client, and that's all they think about, and that's cool because they're really good at it, but everything after the click, not so good, which is great, because that's where all the money is, is after the click, right, I'll give you an example, okay, swear to God, this is right, I literally just took this from a screenshot that a client sent me, so I've got a client in e-com, they sell, this isn't what they sell, I'm just trying to think of something like totally random, they sell manuals for how to clean your rifle, okay, they don't really at all, but it's something similar, okay, so manuals for how to clean your rifle, one of their ads is opt-in, and we'll send you a little PDF about rifle cleaning, okay, when people opt-in, this is email number one of their follow-up sequence, right, so someone opts in, then they go to the download page, and on the download page, there's a little pitch, and most people who opt-in, they don't immediately take the pitch on the download page, so the client is like, okay, I'll follow up with these people, now this is real, all right, this is the stats for email number one, after somebody opts in to get the little lead magnet about cleaning your rifle, and they don't buy the rifle cleaning kit for like eight bucks or whatever it is, right, one email, 50% open rate, 13,000 people by the way, 50% open rate, 31% click, 3 rate, 4.4% order rate, $27,000 in revenue, guess how many emails like it they send after this one, it rhymes with zero, like literally with this client, I'm like, you know, the first thing we got to do is just send this thing like four more times, it's changed the subject, and it was met with resistance by the way, but it's like 27 grand, this is one email, that's it, opt in, get one email at the end, you're done, okay, so like, e-com's different, and it's great, like if you get an e-com client on the line, the hardest thing you're gonna do is, or the most challenging thing you're gonna be faced with is not telling them to do 352,000 things at once, because they ain't doing nothing, you know, I just showed you two of the biggest makeup brands in the world, and you saw the process, like I bought one of them, I didn't press go, but you know, whatever, you saw what it's like, right, and so you see what this other client's doing, like, and this client's damn near eight figures, you know, so let's check out what the ideal e-com process is like, right, so it doesn't matter if they're sending the ad to the product specific lander, or to a presale page, and then to the product specific lander, one thing we can do is if someone goes to leave the product specific landing page, you know, the page had the glop on there, the lipstick, whatever, they go to leave it, we can have a little exit intent pop up, hey, before you go, sign up to get 15% off, or whatever, like anything, right, and then, of course, we should have a two-step order process, so check out step one, both Huda Beauty and Goop, the lovely Italian to go in a paltrow, both of them had two-step order processes, and then that would lead us to a payment page with, God help us, God forbid, you kidding me, order bumps, right, not people who bought this also bought this other stuff, no, it's like actually tried to sell something, you know, the little box or the check mark, it says, hey, one time only, get this thing, add this to your order, whatever, okay, and then we look to see if the people who filled out step one in the order form actually bought anything, now, between me and you, we know that the overwhelming majority of folks who fill out step one ain't gonna buy this stuff because that's how direct response works, so of course, the very first thing we're gonna look for is, is there a card abandoned sequence, which in most cases, probably not, and if it is, we're gonna look to see if it's worth a damn, right, and so in addition to, do you have an order bump, do you have a card abandoned sequence, now we're gonna look to see, okay, do you have a one click up sell, right, because why wouldn't you have a one click up sell, it's crazy, you know, especially in niches like skincare, which is a multi-billion, I think it's a multi-hundred billion dollar a year industry, if I'm not mistaken, I could be wrong on that one, but it's like big, you know, both of those are huge, like people like buying that stuff, so naturally we should have one click up sells, and then just like an info product with a loss leader, we're gonna look and say like, okay, well if they don't take the up sell, let's send them in a deadline sequence to the up sell, and if they do step one and they don't buy, or they do buy, ultimately we need to do email follow-up to sell them more stuff, and you know what, all it needs to be is a click sequence, taking them to a presale page, right, five makeup tips for this summer, six ways to make your lips look fuller, four ways to get rid of bags under your eyes, whatever, and then if they click on it, and they don't buy the thing, the presale page is talking about what do we put them into, a deadline sequence, right, remember from all of our work so far, we know there's really only two types of email sequences in the world, click sequence, and a deadline sequence, right, so no matter what, they're gonna end up in a whole bunch of click sequences and deadline sequences, so the tools to make all this stuff happen, what does your your client need, well Shopify is the number one e-con platform in the world, so that's a good one, it doesn't natively have cool stuff like order bumps and one-click upsells built into it, thankfully good old Ezra Firestone built this thing called Zipify, that's cheap as heck, and it's got that stuff built into it, and it works like on top of Shopify, so it just upgrades Shopify and makes it where they can do stuff like order bumps and one-click upsell, if they don't have a CRM there's a really good one for e-commerce called Drip, that'll measure like how much revenue comes from specific emails and everything, it's really badass, or any other CRM, really all you need is an email thing, as long as like the email system can know when somebody bought something so you can quit bugging them, you're good, and Zapier will do that, so when someone buys something on Shopify, it tags Zapier and Zapier talks to the CRM and says hey tag this guy as a customer, don't send him any hey go buy the stuff emails anymore, and that's it, so if you got an e-con client here's the checklist, right, very very similar to a loss leader checklist, first of all we always look at the big picture numbers, all right, how many visitors you get in a month, what's your ad spend, what's the gross revenue, what's the net revenue after ad spend, how many new customers you're getting, how many new leads, not many e-con people actually even think about leads, but if they're doing a two-step order form then that counts as a lead, so not many e-con people do lead magnet stuff is what I mean, but you know we would look at their step ones here of a two-step order form as a lead, and then what's your average customer value, and it's probably you know depending on what they're doing, it's probably not going to be that high, and what's your cost to acquire one of them, and then you know what's the net you're making per customer, so very very basic simple stuff, same type of numbers we always get, hang on I gotta get some water, and now we've got our component checklist, right, and so thing number one is please tell me you got a two-step order form, right, we need one of those because most people who start to order something don't buy it, so please like let's make sure they got that, if they don't you're stoked, right, that's amazing, and then do they have an order bump, most will not, which is wonderful, do they have a one-time offer, one-click up sell, or a down sell, which in e-commerce, I don't know, but at least like you know multiple one-click up sells, we need that, right, because we want their average customer value to be much higher than it is now, so they probably don't have any of these, and if they do, god forbid, it would be amazing, they've only got one, right, so you got a lot of opportunity there, do they have an exit and pop on the one-click up sell, right, that means that someone's watching the pitch for the one-click up sell and they go to leave, there's a little pop that says wait, hey, here's this in a nutshell, you sure you want to go, really helps conversion on those things, do they have a deadline sequence to the non-buyers of the order bump, do they have a deadline sequence to the non-buyers of these things, are they retargeting the non-buyers, right, so like that's the thing about e-commerce stores, they get a lot of traffic, they're not doing retargeting, they crazy, they probably are doing retargeting, but it's worth looking at to make sure they're doing it right, and of course, do they have a deadline sequence to card abandons, so between us girls, if I got like either of these clients, you know, Goop or what was the other one, Hoot of Beauty, very first thing we would be doing for both of these brands would be order bump, number one, one-click up sell, number two, then deadline sequence to card abandoners, number three, these would be the very first places, you know, that we would focus on and just doing these three things right here probably would revolutionize both of those businesses in terms of making them a lot more money per customer, and they would be like oh my gosh, this is the greatest thing ever, and then you know, we retain them as a client for as long as you wanted them, then we could do all the other stuff, but this is where the lowest hanging fruit is, so the numbers you want to get for any of the stuff they have is pretty simple, right, just like a loss leader framework, we say okay, if you're using a presale page, right, like Hoot of Beauty does, how many people who go to the presale page actually click to the stuff you're selling, that's important to get, the sales page, like you know, the lipstick or whatever, what's the conversion rate on that thing, what's your conversion rate on the step one of your order form, and that's important, you know, I mean because when you click add to cart and then you go to the thing that says give me your name and email and phone number and all that crap, it is like you know, no one's doing that and it needs to be fixed, right, what's the overall order form conversion rate, so meaning of the people that are beginning this process, how many actually buy stuff, you ain't gonna have a damn order bump, I can guarantee you, but if they do, what's the conversion rate on that, they ain't gonna have one of these, but if they do, you get the conversion rate on that, and if they have multiple ones, you get the conversion rate on the multiple ones, and then how many emails are in your follow-up to card abandons if they even have them, and how many emails are in your follow-up for the bump, if they even have them, and how many emails are in your follow-up for the OTOS, right, and in the event, I didn't even put it on here because it's so rare for an e-com store to do lead gen like the example I showed you with my quote unquote gun cleaning manual person. It's not what they sell by the way. It's extremely rare for an e-com person to do that, but in the event they're generating leads like giving away lead magnets, then do that too because good Lord, all the clients got to do is add like go from one email to five emails and they would probably double or triple the revenue from that one little funnel, you know. So these are the numbers that you want to get, you know, just for having them, even though me and you both know it's like, all right, well, let's put an order bump in an OTO and a deadline sequence of card abandons in there and we're probably going to like, you're going to lose your mind here and everyone's going to be happy. But nonetheless, here's other like typical numbers for email sequences if they have them, main follow up, you know, in the event that they're actually like generating leads, card abandoned follow ups is open rate and click through rate as always. Bumped bandons, they ain't going to have them, but if they do, get your open rate and click through rates for there and then any one click up sell, you know, deadline sequences, open and click rates for these, highly unlikely that your clients going to have many of these, if any. But if they do, let's do it because like I showed you with that one client, you know, they've got this freaking 50% open rate, 33% click through rate, one email would brought in $27,000 in revenue. It's like, maybe we should send the second one. What should the second one say? I don't know. How about the exact same thing as the first one, but a different subject line, you know what I mean? Like, there's nothing wrong with the clients. They're just so busy like doing inventory and all of that crap that they don't think about what happens after the click the way you and I do. And then you always have your glaringly obvious observations, which in 99.99999, percent of all e-com cases, it's going to be hot diggity. We should put an order pump and a one click up sell and a pretty aggressive deadline sequence to card abandoners in place there. And that's like the thing. And then the hard part of the engagement becomes dragging that out. And you can even tease the client. Like, oh, man, I'm trying to figure out how I'm going to make it look like it took me a long time to figure this out. I joke around with my clients like that all the time. So that's e-commerce in a nutshell. And man, if you get yourself an e-com client that's doing any volume, you have a pretty low hanging fruit client in terms of being able to really, really help them. So that wraps up this little video here. Super simple, nothing to it. So now probably now you're starting to realize like, oh my gosh, like all this stuff really is kind of doesn't matter what the business type is. It's all fairly formulaic. And the reason behind that is because it is formulaic ain't nothing to it. But to do it. So whether you're doing this stuff for clients or you have an e-com store, this is how you make one work a whole lot better and produce a lot more revenue for you with the exact same amount of traffic and leads and deal flow that you're getting now. So get to work and get yourself some money. Take it easy.
